title: State.things should become parallel arrays (ids + stacked masks) rather than dict-of-dataclasses
body: Under p-types-are-tensor-shape-obligations, a Thing's load-bearing content is its tau_mask, sigma_mask: Bool[Array, 'pool_size'].  Storing 700 Thing dataclasses each wrapping two 1-D bool arrays forces firing_bitmaps to be constructed per step() as a stack operation.  Derivation: if the load-bearing representation is already tensor-shaped and is used as a stacked matrix in every hot path (step() candidate enumeration is firing_bitmaps @ firing_bitmaps.T), then the primary representation IS the stacked matrix and 'a Thing' is a row-view.  Concrete shape: ids: U64[Array, 'n_things']; tau_masks, sigma_masks: Bool[Array, 'n_things pool_size']; metadata (source_path, display) as a structured-dtype array aligned on axis-0.  Intensional Thing identity (d-things-are-named-decls) survives as the metadata row; extensional identity is row-equality on the mask matrices.  Counterexample risk: a scorer that needs per-thing O(1) random access by id without materializing a mask row might argue for the dict; mitigation: id→index is a hash table over the ids array, built once.

title: Oracle pairs should become Int[Array, 'n_pairs 2'] indexing axis-0 of stacked masks, not a frozenset
body: Under p-types-are-tensor-shape-obligations and l-state-things-as-parallel-arrays, things carry numeric axis-0 indices.  Oracle pair usage is iteration-then-reduction: for each pair compute Belnap evidence, sum into a scalar objective.  Derivation: if Things are indexed and the reduction is a sum over pairs, the shape-native form is Int[Array, 'n_pairs 2'] and the evaluation is tau_masks[pairs[:,0]] XOR sigma_masks[pairs[:,1]] reduced along axis-1 — one vectorized operation replacing n_pairs Python iterations.  Objective of type Float[Array, ''] (0-D) falls out naturally.  Set semantics (each unordered pair appears at most once) become a pre-condition maintained by the constructor rather than by the runtime container.  Counterexample risk: if oracle-pair membership tests become frequent, an auxiliary set may be useful; but the current call pattern is reduce, not membership-test.

title: Pool.by_key should become a structured-dtype array, not a tuple of tuples
body: Pool's authoritative index maps K-keys to bit positions in τ/σ masks.  Usage: (1) lookup K-by-bit_index during smoke tests / display; (2) iterate all (K, bit) for scorer-specific enumeration; (3) append on wedge articulation.  Derivation: these are axis-0 operations on a structured-dtype array with fields [('key', 'U256'), ('bit_index', 'u4'), ('grade', 'u1'), ('provenance', 'u1')] — lookup is np.searchsorted / np.where; iteration is slicing; append is np.concatenate.  Critical alignment: pool axis-0 = mask matrix axis-1; adding a K is 'append row to pool + append column to mask matrix', one shape contract enforced across two arrays.  Hash-consing (d-pool-atoms-verbatim-kinds) becomes np.unique on the structured dtype with return_index.  Counterexample risk: some grade / provenance values might need variable-length fields that don't fit structured dtype; mitigation: intern those fields to integer codes, keep the structured array pure fixed-width.

title: S3 action is a numpy axis permutation on a length-3 stacked tensor
body: S3 currently permutes which-of-(τ, σ, κ) is primary via dataclass field shuffling, with the κ = τ ⊕ σ constraint (d-tausigma-type-preserves-constraint) preserved by recomputation.  Derivation under p-hit-and-dependent-types-via-tensor-shape: stack τ, σ, κ along axis-0 into tsk: Bool[Array, '3 pool_size']; S3 acts as tsk[perm, :].  The F₂² constraint is a rank condition on axis-0 (any two rows determine the third via XOR); permutation preserves rank; group composition preserves rank.  The action becomes one line of numpy and is first-class operator-shaped.  Counterexample risk: if some code path needs (τ, σ) as a pair without materializing κ, axis-0 stacking forces materialization; mitigation: κ is the XOR of the other two axes, trivially computed on demand — no storage cost.

title: Trajectory should become a structured-dtype numpy array, not a tuple of dicts
body: Post-Stage-7.0.6 (c-stage-7-0-6-unmaterialize-hash-and-signature) trajectory is load-bearing for termination: run_to_fixed_point tests state.trajectory[-1]['articulated_count'] == 0.  Derivation under p-types-are-tensor-shape-obligations: a dict key is a string not a type; a typo in 'articulated_count' fails silently at runtime.  A structured dtype [('iter', 'u4'), ('articulated_count', 'u4'), ('pool_size_after', 'u4'), ('objective', 'f4'), ('signature_hash', 'u8')] makes the field names part of the type, makes iterator access O(1) field read, and makes the termination predicate's type contract visible on the dtype.  The smoke-test assertions become shape-checks.  Counterexample risk: if trajectory fields vary per iteration (some scorers emit auxiliary fields, some don't), structured dtype is too rigid; mitigation: keep one mandatory base dtype for the hot-path control fields, add a separate auxiliary JSONL for optional scorer output.

title: HDF5 compound dtypes on disk should mirror the in-memory structured dtypes 1:1
body: Under p-storage-matches-data-shape, bulk tensor storage is HDF5; structured records are JSONL.  Given lemmas l-state-things-as-parallel-arrays, l-pool-as-structured-dtype-array, l-trajectory-as-structured-dtype-array, every large in-memory shape is already a numpy structured or homogeneous dtype.  Derivation: HDF5 supports compound dtypes identical in layout to numpy structured dtypes; the simplest on-disk schema is a 1:1 mirror, with pool and masks as separate datasets sharing the 'pool_size' attribute on their parent group.  The dependent-type shape obligation ('pool_size' binds axis-1 of masks and axis-0 of pool) becomes an HDF5 attribute; load-time shape-checks enforce it across files.  Counterexample risk: HDF5's variable-length string columns are slow; mitigation: the only string-valued columns (Thing.display, Thing.source_path) live in the JSONL metadata file, not the HDF5 datasets, per p-storage-matches-data-shape's split.

title: Scorer is a shape contract: (tsk: Bool[3, P], firing: Bool[P, N], i: int, j: int) -> Float[Array, '']
body: Scorer and Objective currently exist as Python Protocol classes carrying intensional identity (p-functions-have-structural-identity) plus an optional firing_bitmaps kwarg (Stage 7.0.5).  Derivation under p-types-are-tensor-shape-obligations: the Protocol class asserts intensional identity (this-Scorer-vs-that-Scorer); what the Protocol says about behavior reduces to a shape-in-shape-out contract.  Inputs: tsk: Bool[Array, '3 pool_size'] (or just (τ, σ) pair as Bool[Array, '2 pool_size']), firing: Bool[Array, 'pool_size n_things'], two bit indices i, j; output: Float[Array, ''].  Composition of scorers becomes np.stack along a new axis-0, producing Float[Array, 'n_scorers'], reducible by whatever aggregator d-articulation-scorer-pluggable prescribes.  Class-shape Protocol stays as the intensional scaffolding; tensor-shape annotation stays as the extensional contract.  Counterexample risk: scorers that require joint distribution across more than two K's need higher-arity signatures; mitigation: Scorer protocol is parameterized by arity (how many bit indices it consumes), remaining a shape contract at each arity.

title: Hash-consing of K becomes np.unique over the pool's structured-dtype array
body: p-bijective-hash-consing requires every K-encoding scheme to be round-trippable, no lossy collisions.  Derivation from l-pool-as-structured-dtype-array: if the pool is a structured-dtype array with a key field, np.unique(pool, axis=0, return_index=True) IS the hash-consing operation — it's bijective on the structured dtype (two rows are equal iff every field matches), returns canonical indices, and runs at numpy speed.  Wedge-articulation's de-duplication (two candidate pairs normalizing to the same canonical Wedge, the Stage 7.0 bug) becomes a shape-level constraint: after concatenating new candidates, np.unique on the new rows.  Counterexample risk: hash-consing over structural normalization (Wedge(a,b) ≡ Wedge(b,a)) requires canonicalization BEFORE the unique call, since structured dtype equality is order-sensitive; mitigation: canonicalization is a sort over the wedge's parent-index pair, applied at row-insert time.

title: S3 lifts from the tsk tensor to K-level operators swap, rotate, compose
body: Under l-s3-as-axis-permutation, S3 acts on the (3, pool_size) tsk tensor as an axis-0 permutation.  Derivation: this tensor-level action lifts to K-level operators.  swap(K) -> K' applies the (τσ) transposition: the resulting K fires in τ where the original fires in σ and vice versa, with κ unchanged.  rotate(K, g: S3) -> K' applies any S3 element as a K-level constructor; compose(K1, K2, g: S3) -> K' applies g then the natural operation (wedge or similar).  These are first-class K constructors alongside Atom, Wedge, ZeroK — the inductive type of K gains three new constructors (or one parameterized by an S3 element).  Consequences: (a) the `channel` parameter on _count_four_cell / firing_bitmaps_of / Thing.fires retires — σ_K(X) = τ_{swap(K)}(X); all scorers read τ; orientation-specific scoring is K-specific not dispatch-specific.  (b) sigma_masks becomes redundant-derivable from tau_masks + orbit-member presence in the pool: if swap(K) is in the pool, its τ-row IS K's σ-row.  Storage can collapse to τ-only.  (c) The S3-orbit structure of the pool becomes first-class metadata: pool entries gain an orbit-id / parent-ref that records their position in the orbit tree.  Counterexample risk: orbit explosion — if every K gets 3-6 orbit members in the pool, pool size grows 3-6×.  Mitigation: orbit members are articulated on demand (like wedges under p-overlap-demands-wedge-articulation), not pre-expanded; a K's orbit member enters the pool only when some scorer or thing observation demands it.

title: General exterior-algebra combinator ≡ S3 operators + AND-wedge (two presentations, one structure)
body: The general exterior-algebra combinator at grade 2 (d-wedge-combinator-general-exterior-algebra) and the S3-operators + AND-wedge pathway (d-wedge-bit-and-of-parents + l-k-level-s3-operators) are extensionally equivalent.  Derivation: τ_{A∧B}(X) = τ_A(X)·σ_B(X) + σ_A(X)·τ_B(X) in GF(2).  The two terms are 'A contributes τ-orientation, B contributes σ-orientation' and the swap.  Under l-k-level-s3-operators, τ_{swap(B)} = σ_B, so the first term equals τ_{A ∧ swap(B)}(X) under AND semantics; the second equals τ_{B ∧ swap(A)}(X).  The GF(2) sum of the two equals the general-combinator value.  Both presentations produce the same Boolean-earning witness structure (both off-diagonals populated per p-boolean-earned-by-both-off-diagonals) and have the same firing predicate at each (thing, K-pair).  The SPPF holds both readings per p-sppf-holds-coexisting-readings.  Choice is pragmatic: the general combinator is a closed-form single-bit-per-wedge computation; the S3+AND pathway is a compositional tower (each S3 orbit member is a pool entry, wedges remain pure AND, the algebra becomes compositional over the enriched pool).  Counterexample risk: pool-size cost of the S3+AND pathway (requires orbit members in pool) vs no-extra-pool-cost of the general combinator (single bit per wedge); which is cheaper depends on whether the orbit structure is exercised broadly (favor S3+AND) or narrowly (favor combinator).  Neither presentation is load-bearing today — the framework currently uses d-wedge-bit-and-of-parents with symmetric atoms, so the asymmetric regime is dormant.  When activated, either presentation suffices; the choice can be made per-deployment.
